items.question_text: Under 9-2-8, what is the controller's default responsibility regarding monitoring or observing aircraft near special interest sites?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'Air traffic controllers have no responsibilities to monitor or observe aircraft in the vicinity of special interest sites unless directed by supervisory/CIC personnel.' The default is no responsibility.

choices.text: Controllers must actively monitor all aircraft within 5 NM of any special interest site at all times.
No responsibilities to monitor or observe unless directed by supervisory/CIC personnel.
Controllers must monitor only nuclear power plants; other sites have no monitoring requirement.
Controllers must report all aircraft within 10 NM of any special interest site to the DEN.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-8, which of the following are examples of special interest sites?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists 'nuclear power plants, power plants, dams, refineries, etc.' as examples of special interest sites.

choices.text: Shopping malls, stadiums, and public parks.
Nuclear power plants, power plants, dams, and refineries.
Airports, VOR stations, and ARTCC facilities.
Military bases, restricted areas, and MOAs only.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-9, what federal regulation prescribes special air traffic rules for designated SATR areas and SFRAs?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-9, special air traffic rules for these areas are listed in 14 CFR part 93 and can be found throughout the NAS.

choices.text: 14 CFR part 91, General Operating and Flight Rules.
14 CFR part 93, Special Air Traffic Rules.
14 CFR part 71, Designation of Class A, B, C, D, and E Airspace.
FAA Order JO 7110.65, Chapter 7.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-9, what is the key distinction between a Special Flight Rules Area (SFRA) and other areas listed in 14 CFR part 93?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-9a, 'Not all areas listed in 14 CFR part 93 are Special Flight Rules Areas, but special air traffic rules apply to all areas designated as SFRA.' The rules apply to all part 93 areas, but not all are designated SFRA.

choices.text: SFRAs have stricter rules than other part 93 areas, with different compliance requirements.
Not all areas listed in 14 CFR part 93 are SFRAs, but special air traffic rules apply to all SFRA-designated areas.
SFRA is a subset of SATR; all SFRAs are SATRs but not all SATRs are SFRAs.
There is no distinction; the terms are used interchangeably throughout part 93.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-10, what must be done immediately upon loss of radio communication or inability to conduct security tracking of an aircraft in the DC SFRA?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-10a1, 'Immediately report all instances of loss of radio communication or the inability to conduct security tracking of an aircraft to the operations supervisor (OS)/CIC and wait for instructions.'

choices.text: Squawk 7600 for the aircraft and terminate security services.
Report to the OS/CIC immediately and wait for instructions.
Broadcast on guard frequency and vector the aircraft out of the SFRA.
Notify the DEN directly and continue tracking using primary radar only.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-10, which separation services do NOT apply to ATC security tracking?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-10a2, 'Basic separation services to aircraft, for example, IFR, SVFR, Class B, Class C, TRSA, do not apply to ATC security tracking.' Security tracking is distinct from separation services.

choices.text: IFR, SVFR, Class B, Class C, and TRSA separation services.
Only IFR separation services; SVFR and Class B/C still apply.
All separation services apply to security tracking without exception.
Only Class B separation services; all others apply to security tracking.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-10, when an aircraft flying too low for radar coverage enters the SFRA and no flight progress strip exists, what information must be recorded?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-10a4, record the 'call sign, transponder code, entry point (for example, north, northeast, east), and time of entry into the SFRA.'

choices.text: Call sign, destination, estimated time en route, and pilot's name.
Call sign, transponder code, entry point, and time of entry into the SFRA.
Only the call sign and entry point; other details are optional.
Call sign, aircraft type, filed altitude, and exit point estimate.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-10, what is the difference in termination procedure for an SFRA aircraft that is landing inside the area versus one that is not?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-10c1-2, non-landing aircraft are told to squawk VFR with frequency change approved upon exit. Landing aircraft are instructed to remain on the assigned transponder code until after landing.

choices.text: Non-landing: squawk VFR, frequency change approved upon exit. Landing: remain on assigned transponder code until after landing.
Both get the same termination: squawk VFR, frequency change approved.
Non-landing gets handoff to next sector; landing aircraft terminates with the tower.
Landing aircraft squawks VFR on short final; non-landing keeps the code until 10 NM outside the SFRA.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-10, which categories of flights are exempt from filing flight plans in the DC SFRA?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-10a5, 'United States military, law enforcement, and aeromedical flights are exempt from filing flight plans.'

choices.text: Civil air patrol, firefighting, and news gathering aircraft.
U.S. military, law enforcement, and aeromedical flights.
All government-operated aircraft regardless of mission.
Experimental, amateur-built, and light sport aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-12, what activities are included in the definition of sensitive government missions?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-12b1, sensitive government missions include 'inflight identification, surveillance, interdiction and pursuit activities conducted by government aircraft for national defense, homeland security, and intelligence or law enforcement purposes.'

choices.text: Inflight identification, surveillance, interdiction, and pursuit activities for national defense, homeland security, and intelligence or law enforcement purposes.
Aerial refueling, formation flying, and celestial navigation training.
Weather reconnaissance, hurricane tracking, and atmospheric sampling.
Flight inspection of NAVAIDs and radar calibration missions.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-12, what must ATC do with information received pertaining to stolen aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-12a4, 'Forward any information received pertaining to stolen aircraft to the OS/CIC for reporting on the Domestic Events Network (DEN).'

choices.text: Issue an immediate alert to all aircraft in the sector.
Forward to the OS/CIC for reporting on the Domestic Events Network (DEN).
Forward directly to local law enforcement without involving OS/CIC.
Record the information in the flight progress strip and take no further action.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-12, regarding exemptions from Title 14 CFR for sensitive government missions, what must each organization's exemption identify?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-12b3, 'Each organization's exemption identifies its responsibilities for notifying ATC of its intent to operate under an exemption before commencing operations.'

choices.text: Its responsibilities for notifying ATC of its intent to operate under the exemption before commencing operations.
The specific 14 CFR parts from which it is exempted.
The geographic boundaries within which the exemption applies.
The names and ranks of personnel authorized to exercise the exemption.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-13, when does MARSA end between the tanker and receiver aircraft?
items.explanation: Per Note 4, MARSA ends 'when the tanker advises ATC that the tanker and receiver aircraft are vertically positioned within the air refueling airspace and ATC advises MARSA is terminated.'

choices.text: When the receiver aircraft reports fuel transfer complete.
When the tanker advises that aircraft are vertically positioned within the refueling airspace and ATC advises MARSA terminated.
When the tanker and receiver have passed the ARCP.
When all aircraft have exited the refueling track.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-13, what happens if ATC issues an altitude or course change after MARSA has been declared but before rendezvous?
items.explanation: Per the note to 9-2-13d, 'Altitude or course changes issued will automatically void MARSA.' This is a critical point — ATC action can unwittingly cancel MARSA protection.

choices.text: MARSA remains in effect but the tanker must acknowledge the change.
MARSA is automatically voided.
The change is rejected and the aircraft maintain their current clearance.
MARSA is suspended temporarily and reinstated after the change is complete.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-13, when may a controller use the altitude vacated during a refueling operation?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-13e, 'Do not use the altitude vacated during the refueling operation until the refueling aircraft has reported reaching the next IFR altitude.'

choices.text: Immediately after the refueling aircraft begins its climb or descent.
After coordinating with the tanker pilot and receiving concurrence.
Not until the refueling aircraft has reported reaching the next IFR altitude.
After 3 minutes have passed since the aircraft vacated the altitude.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-13, in the event of two-way communications failure during aerial refueling, from which altitude does the tanker depart the track?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-13i1, 'The tanker will depart the track from the highest altitude in the block.' The receiver departs from the lowest altitude (9-2-13i2). This vertical separation is built into the lost comm procedure.

choices.text: From the middle altitude in the block.
From the lowest altitude in the block.
From the highest altitude in the block.
From the altitude last assigned by ATC before the failure.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-13, when can nonradar routes be furnished to aerial refueling aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-13f, 'Furnish nonradar routes only after the refueling aircraft have passed the ARCP.' Vectors and alternative altitudes may be furnished at any time, but nonradar routes are restricted until after ARCP.

choices.text: At any time upon tanker pilot request.
Only before the aircraft reach the ARIP.
Only after the refueling aircraft have passed the ARCP.
Nonradar routes may never be furnished to aerial refueling aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-13, what transponder code and for how long must aircraft squawk before departing the refueling track during a communications failure?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-13i3, 'Aircraft will squawk 7600 for at least 2 minutes prior to departing the track.'

choices.text: Squawk 7700 for at least 2 minutes before departure.
Squawk 7600 for at least 2 minutes prior to departing the track.
Squawk 7600 continuously from the onset of failure until landing.
Squawk 7700 for 1 minute, then 7600, before departing.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-14, what route-of-flight requirements apply to flight plans involving operations above FL 600?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-14b, the route must be defined by at least one high altitude fix within each ARTCC area, and entry/exit points of turns of 90 degrees or more must be designated.

choices.text: At least one high altitude fix within each ARTCC area, with entry and exit points designated for turns of 90 degrees or more.
A fix at every 50 NM interval along the route regardless of ARTCC boundaries.
Only the departure and destination fixes are required; en route fixes are optional.
The same route requirements as subsonic flights with no special fixes needed.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-14, what airspace must be protected laterally during a supersonic turn?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-14f2, for supersonic aircraft turns, protect 75 miles on the overflown side and 25 miles on the other side. For subsonic turns: 34 miles on overflown side, 25 miles on the other side.

choices.text: 75 miles on the overflown side and 25 miles on the other side.
34 miles on the overflown side and 25 miles on the other side.
25 miles on each side of the route centerline, same as straight flight.
50 miles on each side of the route centerline for all turns.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-14, what must be included in the route of flight for operations above FL 600 regarding timing?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-14c, 'Elapsed times from takeoff to the first fix in each ARTCC area must be included in the route of flight.'

choices.text: Elapsed times from takeoff to the first fix in each ARTCC area.
Estimated times en route for every fix in the flight plan.
Only the estimated time of arrival at the destination.
Fuel endurance times at each fix along the route.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-14, what does ATC approval of the flight plan for a flight above FL 600 also approve?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-14e, 'Approval of the flight plan indicates approval of both route and flight levels (if stated) including operations below FL 600 (aerial refueling).'

choices.text: Only the cruise portion above FL 600.
Both route and flight levels, including operations below FL 600 such as aerial refueling.
Route only; flight levels must be approved separately by each ARTCC.
Route and flight levels, but operations below FL 600 require a separate clearance.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-14, why is radar separation the preferred method for separating a subsonic aircraft from another subsonic or supersonic aircraft above FL 600?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'Vectoring supersonic aircraft seriously affects the range and mission objectives.' Descending the lower aircraft for vertical separation is the primary method since the higher aircraft may not climb rapidly enough.

choices.text: Because supersonic aircraft cannot accept altitude changes once above FL 600.
Because vectoring supersonic aircraft seriously affects range and mission objectives, and the higher aircraft may not climb rapidly enough for vertical separation.
Because radar separation is the only authorized method above FL 600; vertical separation is prohibited.
Because subsonic aircraft above FL 600 cannot be tracked using non-radar methods.

items.situation: A pilot reports observing an aircraft circling at low altitude near a nuclear power plant in your sector. The supervisor has not issued any monitoring directive.
items.question_text: What is your responsibility regarding this report?

choices.text: Immediately relay the report to supervisory/CIC personnel.
Ignore the report unless the supervisor specifically directs you to monitor the area.
Vector the suspicious aircraft away from the power plant and notify the DEN.
Treat it as routine traffic and take no special action.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-8a, immediately relay any reports regarding unusual aircraft activities near special interest sites to supervisory/CIC personnel. The note clarifies that controllers have no default monitoring responsibility, but reports received must be relayed.

items.situation: You are providing security tracking to a VFR aircraft in the DC SFRA. The aircraft is not landing at an airport within the SFRA and is approaching the SFRA boundary. The aircraft has been on an assigned transponder code.
items.question_text: How do you terminate security services for this aircraft?

choices.text: Provide security services until the aircraft exits the area, then advise squawk VFR and frequency change approved.
Terminate services at the boundary and tell the aircraft to squawk standby.
Instruct the aircraft to remain on the assigned transponder code until after landing at its destination outside the SFRA.
Hand off to the next sector without any special termination instruction.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-10c1, if not landing within the area, provide security services until the aircraft exits, then advise squawk VFR and frequency change approved. The 'remain on assigned code until landing' instruction (9-2-10c2) applies only to aircraft landing inside the area.
items.para_context: ATC SECURITY SERVICES FOR THE WASHINGTON, DC, SPECIAL FLIGHT RULES

items.question_text: A law enforcement aircraft requests assistance in locating a suspect aircraft. A controller is concerned about workload. Under 9-2-12, what must be provided?

choices.text: Assist in locating the suspect aircraft when requested, while ensuring assistance does not compromise separation minima or safety.
Deny the request because locating suspect aircraft is not an ATC function.
Provide assistance only if the law enforcement aircraft declares an emergency.
Transfer the request to the DEN and take no further action.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-12a3, when requested, assist law enforcement in locating suspect aircraft. However, 9-2-12a2 limits this: assistance must not compromise approved separation minima or place aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.

items.situation: Aerial refueling is in progress. MARSA has been declared between the tanker and receiver. The tanker has not yet completed rendezvous with the receiver. Another controller suggests issuing a heading change to the tanker to avoid a weather cell.
items.question_text: How should the heading change request be handled?

choices.text: Avoid issuing course or altitude changes prior to rendezvous, because such changes will automatically void MARSA.
Issue the heading change immediately because weather avoidance takes priority over MARSA.
Issue the heading change but tell the tanker that MARSA remains in effect.
Ask the receiver to accept the heading change instead, since receiver course changes do not void MARSA.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-13d and its note, after MARSA has been declared, avoid issuing course or altitude changes prior to rendezvous, because altitude or course changes issued will automatically void MARSA.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-14, what vertical separation minimum is used for operations above FL 600 in lieu of the normal Chapter 4 value?

choices.text: 2,000 feet.
5,000 feet.
4,000 feet.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-14f1, in lieu of paragraph 4-5-1 Vertical Separation Minima, use 5,000 feet for operations above FL 600.
